title: Today's Tech Brief: GOG apologizes for emailing people Nazi symbols
summary: A gaming platform's email mishap, Microsoft's AI ambitions, and the evolving role of artificial intelligence
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for GOG apologizes for emailing people Nazi symbols
imageCaption: GOG apologizes for emailing people Nazi symbols (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. I am looking at some interesting developments in the tech world, and I'm excited to share them with you. Today, we've got a couple of stories that caught my eye - a bit of a mishap from a gaming platform and some big ambitions from Microsoft's AI team.

First up, GOG, a popular gaming platform, is apologizing for a serious mistake. They sent out a newsletter that included the double S symbol associated with the Nazi Schutzstaffel. This symbol has been linked to hate groups and is understandably a sensitive topic. The company quickly apologized and assured users it was an error. My read is that it's a stark reminder of the power of symbols and the importance of being thoughtful in our communications. It's easy to see how something like this could have been missed, but that doesn't make it any less important to address.

Next, Microsoft is taking its AI ambitions to the next level. Their AI tool, currently known as Copilot, is evolving to become more autonomous. The idea is that this AI can take on more responsibilities, essentially 'taking the wheel' in certain tasks. This shift towards more autonomous AI raises interesting questions about trust, security, and the future of work. I mean, what does it mean for AI to 'take the wheel'? Are we heading towards a future where machines are making more decisions for us?

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
